findings.summary: Both frameworks establish a phased, gradual implementation approach for open finance/API adoption to allow orderly industry adoption.
findings.label: aligns-with

source_anchors.text:
Building an open finance ecosystem will be a multi-year journey and requires
collective action from all stakeholders to ensure its orderly and secure
implementation. As such, BNM will gradually phase in the requirements in this
Policy Document. This allows for orderly adoption by the industry, while enabling
customers and other ecosystem stakeholders to develop familiarity and
confidence in open finance.

target_anchors.text:
The implementation of Open API would bring many
benefits and efficiency gain to the banking industry and
to its customers. Feedback from the banking industry
indicates that it is desirable to implement Open APIs to
provide better services to satisfy the demands of its
customers. Banks are therefore expected to implement the
Open API framework in the timeline set out in the
framework. The HKMA will monitor the implementation
closely, and act accordingly to ensure market adoption
and encourage use cases.

The scope and selection of Open API functions are the key and
the first step because the correct identification could lead to early
implementation. At the same time, the industry's desire for a
progressive and step-by-step approach to implement Open APIs
beginning with a less risky set of functions is recognised.

Owing to the increased sensitivity of data and therefore the
increased risks involved in each subsequent category, the
protective measures required will be more complicated. Therefore,

a four-phased approach matching the four categories of Open API,
is recommended.

It is envisaged that the deployment of Product and service
information will be similar to an open data initiative which the
information technology industry is already familiar with. The
implementation and timeline for the deployment should therefore
be relatively simple and short.

Subsequent phases of Open APIs would involve more
sophisticated technology, security and authentication
infrastructure, and therefore may require more development
efforts. However, as banks would have accumulated experience in
prior phases, timelines of the subsequent phases are not expected
to be disproportionately long.

Based on the comments received from the consultation, the
proposed six-month timeline after the publication of this
framework for launching Product and service information
(referred as Phase I) Open APIs by banks remains. The proposed
12-month timeline for Subscription and new applications
(referred as Phase II) is relaxed to 12 - 15 months to allow the
industry more time for preparation.

For the subsequent phases, Open APIs would allow even more
innovative and convenient services. As the complexity and risk of
opening up data of all customers increase, the technology and
infrastructure to support, monitor and secure the Open API access
become more complex and critical. The HKMA will therefore
closely monitor the situation and take into consideration local and
international developments, and decide the timeline with the
industry during the coming 12 months.

findings.scope_note: BNM phases in binding requirements per Appendix 2 timelines; HKMA phases by risk-based category with recommended (not mandatory) timelines.

findings.summary: BNM mandates open finance participation for mandated FSPs with binding standards and enforcement, whereas HKMA deliberately adopts a collaborative, non-mandatory approach.
findings.label: differs-on

source_anchors.text:
S 9.1
The obligations of a mandated FSP as specified under paragraph 8.4 shall
commence in accordance with the timeline specified in Appendix 2.

S 9.2
A mandated FSP shall commence its obligation under paragraph 8.6 to provide
document submission and single account view interfaces by 1 January 2028 or

the date on which the FSP's obligations under paragraph 8.4 commence,
whichever is later.

S 8.1
An FSP may participate2 as a data provider or a data consumer on an open
finance platform, subject to meeting relevant requirements in this Policy
Document and that specified by the platform operator.

sharing by a mandated FSP whose obligation to commence as a data provider
falls on or after 1 January 2028 shall include both individual and SME customers.

target_anchors.text:
The HKMA has taken note of the mandatory approach
adopted by some jurisdictions such as the EU, the UK
and Australia but has decided that a collaborative and
phased approach is an appropriate approach for Hong
Kong for the time being. The HKMA will monitor the
progress of Open API implementation in Hong Kong and
further consider the need for new regulatory measures if
necessary.

The framework is intended to be high-level in order
to allow banks the flexibility in implementing Open
API as part of their strategy. The HKMA does not
wish to prescribe how banks should adopt Open API
down to implementation steps.

findings.scope_note: BNM 8.1 participation as data provider/consumer is voluntary generally; the mandatory element applies specifically to mandated FSPs per Appendix 1. HKMA explicitly noted but declined the EU/UK/Australia mandatory approach.

findings.summary: HKMA specifies liability arrangements between banks and TSPs to protect customers in cases of loss, which BNM's document does not cover.
findings.label: silent-on

target_anchors.text:
In line with the relevant clause in the Code of Banking Practice, a
customer should not be responsible for any direct loss suffered by
him/her as a result of unauthorised transactions conducted

Given the wide range of service offerings made possible with
Open API, liabilities between banks and TSPs will be dependent
on the actual mode of operation. Banks and TSPs should
therefore define and agree a clear liability and settlement
arrangement to protect customers in the cases of loss, and
communicate clearly to customers.

findings.summary: HKMA requires banks to publish lists of partnering TSPs and monitor for scams/impersonation to protect the public, an area not addressed by BNM.
findings.label: silent-on

target_anchors.text:
In order to ensure public trust and consumer protection, banks are
expected to publish a list of partnering TSPs and their relevant
products (such as mobile apps or websites) for Phase II and
beyond. The HKMA may work with the industry to centrally
provide to the public with a list of partnering TSPs and their
relevant products. In this connection, banks should provide
updates to the list in a timely manner.

In addition, banks should monitor the Internet regularly to see if
there are third party websites, apps and similar scams which
purported to be operated by the banks' partnering TSPs under the
Open API framework or claimed to be partnering with the banks
when they are not. Whenever banks become aware of these cases,
banks should notify promptly their customers and the public
through issuing press releases (or similarly effective means).

Liability

findings.scope_note: HKMA publication obligation applies to Phase II and beyond.

findings.summary: Both share the vision of using open finance/APIs to spur innovation, improve customer experience and promote financial inclusion.
findings.label: aligns-with

source_anchors.text: Open finance plays a pivotal role in advancing this vision by offering customers a safer and more structured framework for sharing their financial information compared to existing data-sharing arrangements in Malaysia. By enabling consent-driven information sharing, open finance provides customers with greater control over their personal financial information, allowing them to actively determine and manage how their information is shared, accessed and used. Beyond empowering customers, open finance unlocks data-driven innovation that enables customers to make better-informed decisions about their finances, facilitates the delivery of more personalised financial services and promotes greater financial inclusion as illustrated in Diagram 1. Benefits for consumers Benefits to financial industry Financial inclusion and access Better informed customer decisions Better user experience and convenience Opportunities to grow business and partnerships Operational efficiency Risk management and fraud detection *Use cases listed are for illustrative purposes only and are not exhaustive nor indicative of endorsement from BNM. .

As the financial sector continues to evolve in the digital age, the volume of
customer information processed has grown significantly alongside the
increasing shift of transactions to digital platforms. This underscores the
importance of having strong infrastructures that enable the secure access,
sharing and processing of financial information, which is a critical foundation for
maintaining trust and resilience in an increasingly digital financial system.

target_anchors.text:
ensure the competitiveness and relevance of the banking
sector;

provide a secure, controlled and convenient operating
environment to allow banks and their partners1 (called
third party service providers, or TSPs, in this
framework), to work together and develop
innovative/integrated banking services that improve
customer experience; and

The implementation of Open API would bring many
benefits and efficiency gain to the banking industry and
to its customers. Feedback from the banking industry
indicates that it is desirable to implement Open APIs to
provide better services to satisfy the demands of its
customers. Banks are therefore expected to implement the
Open API framework in the timeline set out in the
framework. The HKMA will monitor the implementation
closely, and act accordingly to ensure market adoption
and encourage use cases.

findings.summary: Both provide for document submission and single-account-view use cases, though BNM makes them binding obligations with fixed timelines while HKMA lists them as high-level API function categories.
findings.label: differs-on

source_anchors.text:
interfaces that allow customers to direct the platform to request relevant
information from other data providers through the platform in a machine-
readable and standardised format,4 in lieu of traditional methods of
submitting documents (e.g. manual upload, emailing documents), for
relevant processes (e.g. customer onboarding, on-going due diligence);
and

b) Single account view, where the mandated FSP offers, as part of its internet
banking and mobile banking interfaces, the option for a customer to
display relevant information from their accounts with other data providers
through the platform.

G 8.7 An FSP participating in an open finance platform as a data consumer may
consider adopting principles of inclusivity, accessibility and usability when
designing customer-facing interfaces. Particular attention may be given to
underserved segments (including individuals with basic digital literacy and
persons with disabilities) who may face challenges with overly sophisticated
interfaces.

S 9.1
The obligations of a mandated FSP as specified under paragraph 8.4 shall
commence in accordance with the timeline specified in Appendix 2.

S 9.2
A mandated FSP shall commence its obligation under paragraph 8.6 to provide
document submission and single account view interfaces by 1 January 2028 or

the date on which the FSP's obligations under paragraph 8.4 commence,
whichever is later.

target_anchors.text:
Subscription
and
new
applications
for
product/service - Customer acquisition process such as
allowing online submissions/application of credit cards,
loans or other bank products;

Account information - Retrieval and alteration (where
applicable) of account information (balance, transaction
history, limits, payment schedules, etc.) of authenticated
customers for stand-alone or aggregated views; and

High-level Open API functions have been selected on the
basis of their potential benefits to banks and customers,
and identified in order of priority.

findings.scope_note: BNM single account view / document submission obligation commences by 1 January 2028 or when para 8.4 obligations commence, whichever is later, and covers individual and SME customers except credit card.

findings.summary: Both require board/senior management or governance oversight, but BNM imposes explicit board oversight and escalation duties while HKMA relies on committee-led continuous development.
findings.label: goes-beyond

source_anchors.text:
S 7.1
The board and senior management shall exercise effective oversight of an FSP's
implementation of arrangements relating to open finance to ensure its efficient,
resilient and secure operations.

S 7.2
For the avoidance of doubt, the board and senior management shall satisfy key
responsibilities arising from an FSP's participation in an open finance
arrangement, as may be applicable in relevant policy documents outlined in
paragraph 6.1.

ensure that material issues and concerns relating to the FSP's open
finance arrangements are promptly escalated to the board, which include
but are not limited to breaches of customer information security and
confidentiality.

target_anchors.text:
The HKAB have expressed full support for Open APIs as one of
the HKMA's seven initiatives for "A New Era of Smart Banking"
and confirmed that a committee will work on this continuous
development of Open APIs with the HKMA to support the
changing needs of the industry as technology evolves.

Once Open APIs have been implemented by banks, there needs to
be a body to review the relevance of the architecture, security and
data standards on an ongoing basis. The body may also take on
other industry-wide tasks, such as coordination and consumer
education, where needed.

In the longer term, if harmonisation of Open $A P I$ functions is
desired by the industry, the body can also take on this task to
work with the industry to achieve interoperability.

findings.scope_note: HKMA's governance body is industry/committee-level continuous-development oversight, not firm-level board accountability, so this is a genuine coverage gap on our side going beyond.

findings.summary: BNM requires customer breach handling and response plans with escalation and remedial procedures, which HKMA does not cover beyond scam monitoring.
findings.label: goes-beyond

source_anchors.text:
breach handling and response plan in the event of theft, loss, misuse or
unauthorised access, modification or disclosure of customer information
associated with an open finance arrangement. The plan must, at a minimum,
include escalation procedures and a clear line of responsibility to contain the
customer information breach and take remedial actions.
assistance, complaints handling and redress mechanisms.

findings.summary: Both leverage industry/international standards and interoperable, standardised formats to ensure ease of implementation.
findings.label: aligns-with

source_anchors.text:
interfaces that allow customers to direct the platform to request relevant
information from other data providers through the platform in a machine-
readable and standardised format,4 in lieu of traditional methods of
submitting documents (e.g. manual upload, emailing documents), for
relevant processes (e.g. customer onboarding, on-going due diligence);
and

An FSP participating in an open finance arrangement shall also comply with
technology and operational requirements of the open finance platform for which
the FSP is a member. This may include, but is not limited to, requirements in
relation to the user journey, API specifications, uptime requirements, operational
procedures and requirements for independent review relating to the operational
resilience of its technology functions.

target_anchors.text:
Existing international or industry practices have been
leveraged in the framework in order to ensure ease of
implementation and speedy development of Open APIs.

Taking into account the practices and experience of the UK,
Singapore and Japan, and the feedback during the consultation
exercise, high-level core-banking Open API functions are
specified for deployment and further set out in Annex $\mathrm { A } .$

To help speedy and timely implementation, banks may decide
their own data specification. Banks, however, should publish such
definition (often called "data dictionary") using industry practices
normally in use, such as OpenAPI Specification (also known as
Swagger).

findings.summary: HKMA requires banks to submit implementation road maps to the regulator and plans to publish summaries, a specific supervisory reporting expectation not present in BNM's framework.
findings.label: silent-on

target_anchors.text:
In this connection, banks are expected to provide the HKMA with
road maps of Open API implementation (Product and service
information within two months, and Subscription and $n e w$
applications within eight months from the publication of this
framework). Banks are also expected to explain how their road
maps meet the general scope described in Annex $\mathrm { A }$ (and in the
case of gaps, the reasons).

The HKMA plans to publish a summary (covering at least the
functions to implement and their timelines) of the road maps for
individual banks so that the industry can better plan for service
offerings. The publication will also reduce the workload of banks
in providing such information to individual prospective TSPs.

Banks are always welcome to implement Open APIs ahead of
time or implement functions that have not been specified in this
framework.

findings.summary: HKMA recommends providing a testing environment with (possibly artificial) customer data and a single-point-of-reference dashboard/repository of Open APIs to facilitate TSP adoption, which BNM does not address.
findings.label: silent-on

target_anchors.text:
A single point of reference (may also be known as a
repository or a dashboard) of all Open APIs offered by
banks will facilitate ease of access by TSPs. During the
discussion process, some banks suggested that it would
be desirable for the Data Studio of the Hong Kong
Science and Technology Parks to take up this dashboard
role. Hence, it is recommended that all Open APIs from
Phase I onwards are listed under the Data Studio.

Furthermore, banks should provide examples and testing
environment with data (including artificial customer data
if necessary) to assist TSPs in using their Open APIs.

findings.summary: Both anchor data sharing within a broader legal/regulatory data-protection framework, BNM via the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 and HKMA via applicable personal data protection laws and guidance.
findings.label: aligns-with

source_anchors.text:
This Policy Document sets out the regulatory framework for open finance which
seeks to ensure that it is implemented in an efficient, resilient and secure
fashion. In addition to ensuring that the sharing of data is conducted in full
compliance with the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 and other relevant data
privacy and protection requirements, the Policy Document seeks to promote
responsible data sharing that strikes an appropriate balance between financial
innovation and prudent management of risks.

of personal data is neither carried out by banks
nor directly related to bank business, and (iii)
comply with the applicable laws and guidance
on the protection of personal data; and

unsupported.summary: Both address secure data transmission and authentication, but BNM references its Risk Management in Technology policy while HKMA prescribes specific technologies (X.509, TLS, OAuth 2.0).
unsupported.label: differs-on

unsupported.summary: Both scale security/protection requirements to the sensitivity of data being accessed.
unsupported.label: aligns-with

unsupported.summary: BNM sets detailed, binding consent requirements (voluntary, revocable, explicit, no bundled/blanket consent, consent dashboards, expiry periods) that HKMA's high-level framework does not cover.
unsupported.label: goes-beyond

unsupported.summary: BNM requires deletion/secure disposal of customer information upon consent expiry, which HKMA's framework does not address.
unsupported.label: goes-beyond

unsupported.summary: Both require third-party/TSP governance and safeguards over data handling, but HKMA elaborates due-diligence models (central entity, common baseline) while BNM cross-references outsourcing and customer-information policies.
unsupported.label: aligns-with

unsupported.summary: BNM requires data consumers to apply safeguards at least equivalent to those for their own customer information; HKMA does not impose an equivalence standard on TSP-received data.
unsupported.label: goes-beyond

unsupported.summary: BNM encourages inclusive, accessible interface design for underserved segments (basic digital literacy, persons with disabilities), which HKMA's framework does not address.
unsupported.label: goes-beyond
